# Questions in Livestream 208

Live Stream #208: Hysterical Context Error

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:03</a> | Did Egyptians actually think the brain was useless? | No. They did not know exactly what it did, but medical texts and surgery show they knew brain injury affected the person. | He points to the Edwin Smith Papyrus and trepanation evidence: Egyptians performed brain surgery and saw that brain malfunction affected the whole person. He also rejects the common hook-through-the-nose mummification story, saying the brain was usually left in the skull and dehydrated there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:47</a> | Did Egyptians know about or use metal plates after brain surgery? | He has not seen evidence for metal plates, wood, or other replacements; the cases he knows suggest skin closure and healing over time. | He says the archaeological evidence does not show metal, wood, or another filler in the skull opening. The apparent healing and bone knitting would actually be interfered with by a plate, so he thinks they likely closed the skin or left the wound to heal without a hard insert. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:32</a> | After David told his secular friends he was a Christian, were any of them surprised? | Not really. It came up naturally in conversation rather than as a dramatic announcement. | He says he did not make a formal announcement to friends; religion came up casually in ordinary conversation, so there was no dramatic revelation. He adds that because it was not presented with drama, people generally did not respond with drama, though he mentions one later acquaintance who was surprised. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=1157" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:17</a> | Considering how far back Jericho's history goes, is it odd that nothing from Abraham to Jacob mentions it? | No. Jericho guarded the Jordan fords, and Abraham and Jacob were not using that route. | He describes Jericho as a fortress guarding the low crossing points of the Jordan. Abraham came into the land from the north through Syria, Jacob was already within Canaan, and the patriarchs were pasturing flocks around places such as Hebron rather than taking animals into the Jordan valley. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=1312" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:52</a> | Have book critics said anything about the novel? | Yes. One Christian fiction reviewer had high praise for it. | He says one Christian fiction reviewer on Twitter reviewed the novel alongside another work and praised it as entertaining, thoughtful, educational, and suitable for Christian audiences. Falk also notes that his prior fiction-writing experience helped him avoid some specialist-author pitfalls named in the review. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=1632" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:12</a> | Is Ancient Egypt academia really so backbiting? | Egyptology has some of that, but theology and seminary academia can be worse. | In the context of the book review's joke about backstabbing academics, he says Egyptology has some academic backbiting, but theology and Christian seminary settings can be much worse. He treats the novel's socially awkward academic cast as exaggerated but rooted in real academic behavior. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=1765" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:25</a> | Is the novel actually your take on ancient aliens? | Sort of, as fiction and a what-if premise, but not as a claim that ancient aliens were real. | He says ancient aliens can be a useful fictional premise if the reader keeps fiction and reality separate. He explicitly rejects the idea that aliens built the pyramids, but thinks the what-if scenario can make interesting science fiction about humanity, space exploration, alien races, and spiritual beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=1931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:11</a> | Any book recommendations for New Testament use of the Old Testament? | He did not have a single recommendation and discussed an unrealized Carson/Longman project that would have been useful. | He says he does not know a strong single resource offhand. He recalls hearing about a planned D. A. Carson and Tremper Longman project that would have traced how New Testament passages use the Old Testament, but says it apparently never came to fruition. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=2057" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:17</a> | What would the Israelites have done during 40 years in the desert? | They still had tents, families, clothing, livestock, and camp duties; manna did not make the wilderness years idle. | He rejects the idea that manna meant idleness: gathering manna itself was work, and the people still had flocks to water and feed, tents to make, clothes to maintain, and ordinary camp life to manage. He frames the wilderness life as tedious rather than boring or effortless. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=2224" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:04</a> | Would there have been regular sacrifices during the wilderness wandering? | Yes, at least when the tabernacle was set up. | He answers briefly that sacrifices would have occurred because the tabernacle existed. His qualification is that this applies when the tabernacle was set up, since that would be the setting for sacrificial practice during the wandering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=2249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:29</a> | What about the verse saying their clothing and sandals did not wear out? | He takes it as God continuing to provide for them, not as meaning every original item stayed physically unchanged. | He says the statement should not be read naively as every original pair of shoes and garment physically lasting forty years. He takes the point to be that God kept supplying replacements and preserving them from loss, so they had what they needed throughout the journey. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=2328" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:48</a> | Do you take the 40 years in the wilderness as historical or allegorical? | He takes it as idiomatic and approximate, not necessarily an exact count. | He does not classify the number as either a strict historical count or an allegory. Since forty is idiomatic, he thinks the actual period may have been close to forty years, perhaps something like thirty-eight or forty-two, but should not be overread as exact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=2421" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:21</a> | Do we know of ancient Egyptian or other ancient Near Eastern kings with as many wives as Solomon? | Yes. Ramesses is given as the main example. | He identifies Ramesses as an example of a king with comparable numbers of wives. He explains that royal wives often came through treaty marriages while concubines functioned differently, and he notes the practical burden of housing, feeding, and managing so many women in a royal household. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=2831" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:11</a> | My sister returned to Christ and her children are seeing shadowy figures. Any recommendations? | Praise God and pray; if it continues, also consider possible medical or psychological explanations. | His first counsel is spiritual: praise God, pray, and use praise because demonic entities would not like praise of God or Christ. He also cautions not to ignore medical explanations such as medication effects, sleep paralysis, or rare seizure conditions, so if prayer does not resolve it, the family should investigate those possibilities too. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=3183" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:03</a> | If someone in the ancient Near East thought the gods were angry and could not appease them, would he ask priests to intercede? | Yes. People could ask priests to make offerings or sacrifices on their behalf. | He says this did happen when people thought they had angered a deity and could not appease it themselves. They might go to priests for offerings or sacrifices, and priests were willing because they understood the gods as capable of being vindictive. He says the pattern is clearer in Mesopotamian records but also appears in Egyptian material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=3257" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:17</a> | What is the best explanation of the prosperity gospel? | In short: enough faith supposedly obligates God to give health, wealth, or whatever is desired. | He summarizes prosperity teaching as the claim that faith gives people what they want: money, health, luxury goods, or success. In that system, sickness or poverty is blamed on insufficient faith, making God seem obligated to provide whatever the believer imagines strongly enough. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=3337" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:37</a> | How did the prosperity gospel start, and how old is it? | It is not very old; he connects it mainly to aberrant 1960s Pentecostal revival teaching and televangelism. | He traces it to aberrant strands of Pentecostal revival teaching in the 1960s and says televangelism gave it momentum because television ministry required large amounts of money. In his account, prosperity preachers could leverage fear and greed, then blame disappointed followers for lacking faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=3651" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:51</a> | What is the meaning of dominion over fish and birds in Genesis 1:28? | It is about humanity ruling as God's representative within the created order. | He frames Genesis 1 as God creating an untamed world and giving humanity dominion so the world could be ordered like Eden. Dominion is therefore not mere exploitation but a mandate to rule under God by bringing order to creation and extending the garden pattern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=3953" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:53</a> | In Genesis 39:8-9, was Joseph saying the sin would be adultery or abuse of blessings and trust? | Both are involved: it would be adultery, a betrayal of Potiphar's trust, and a violation of Egyptian social expectations. | He says Joseph is directly talking about adultery, but also about betraying Potiphar's trust after being placed over the household. From the Egyptian side, Joseph's slave status adds another layer because the act would cross social strata and violate expected social order. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=4206" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:06</a> | In Genesis 39:12, what garment would Joseph have worn? | Likely a light linen robe or wrap, suitable for Egyptian conditions. | He initially suggests a light linen robe or wrap used by Egyptian overseers, something cool in the day and warm enough at night. He then qualifies that the word is broad and a kilt may be more likely, since men of status and even higher-status slaves commonly wore kilts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=4453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:13</a> | What does Malachi 2:16 mean, and how should it be translated? | He reads it as condemning divorce and treacherous pretexts, with the idiom meaning something like "I hate sending away." | Working through the Hebrew aloud, he says the phrase uses an idiom or euphemism for divorce, literally along the lines of hating "sending away" or "putting away." He also connects the surrounding language about covering a garment with violence or wrong to treacherous dealing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=4963" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:43</a> | Where are the Benjaminites in the New Testament? | He corrects this to Gadites and points to Luke 8. | He says the reference is not to Benjaminites but to Gadites, the tribe east of the Jordan. He connects this to Luke 8, where Jesus crosses to the country opposite Galilee near Gadite territory and the account includes swine, fitting a region outside ordinary Jewish settlement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=5296" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:16</a> | Is pig leather a thing? | Yes, but it would be unclean for Israelites; modern "pigskin" footballs are usually cow leather. | He says pig leather exists and has been used historically, but an Israelite concerned with ritual cleanness should not handle it because pig remains unclean. He adds that modern footballs called pigskins are normally made from cow leather, so the nickname should not be taken literally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=5344" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:04</a> | Did Nefertiti take Neferneferuaten as a throne name? | He describes this as controversial and says many Egyptologists now identify Neferneferuaten as one of Nefertiti's daughters. | He says the issue is disputed, but that many Egyptologists now think Neferneferuaten was not Nefertiti herself but one of her daughters. In his summary, Nefertiti may have used court influence to put a daughter on the throne briefly, ruling through her because the daughter had a stronger claim than Nefertiti did. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=5524" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:04</a> | Thoughts on the Dynasty 5 tomb discovery? | He has no firm opinion yet and wants official publication details before commenting. | He says he has only seen a brief news notice and wants an official publication before evaluating the discovery. In particular, he would want inscriptions, regnal-year data, or other concrete details before forming a view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=5579" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:59</a> | Are skeptics becoming more skeptical without warrant? | He says that is plausible and connects it to atheism losing cultural and argumentative momentum. | He sees this as part of atheism moving from a once ordinary position into a more ideological and partisan phase after New Atheism lost momentum. In his view, once a movement becomes doctrinaire, warrant matters less and argument can devolve into refusal rather than reasoned debate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=6021" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:21</a> | What double offense did Moses commit that kept him from entering Canaan? | He says it need not be a double offense; Moses misrepresented God by acting angry when God was not. | He contrasts Moses with Miriam, where he sees a clearer double offense. For Moses, he says the decisive sin was serious enough on its own: by striking the rock in anger, Moses represented God as angry when God was not, which he connects to taking God's name in vain by acting as a poor representative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=6203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:23</a> | What makes Abraham a prophet? | Abraham received theophany and prophecy, including promises about a son and many nations. | He says Abraham was visited by the angel of the Lord and received divine prophecies. The examples he gives are the promise of a son, the promise that Abraham would be father of many nations, and the prophecy that his descendants would be in Egypt for four hundred years. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=6330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:30</a> | I do not like wine. What do I do for communion? | It depends on the denomination; some require wine, while others permit grape juice, water, or exceptions. | He says the answer depends heavily on the church body: some traditions insist on wine, while many evangelical churches use grape juice and some traditions allow water or make exceptions, especially for alcoholics. His practical answer is therefore to follow the rules and allowances of the denomination. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=6466" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:46</a> | Can you explain Daniel 11:37? | He sees the verse as probably pointing beyond Antiochus IV toward an antichrist figure who rejects inherited gods and worships military power. | He describes Daniel 11 as a difficult prophecy involving kings of north and south and possibly the abomination of desolation. In verses 36-40 he sees a figure who magnifies himself above every god, rejects ancestral gods, and honors a "god of fortresses," which he takes as language likely pointing past Antiochus IV toward an antichrist figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=7203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:03</a> | Explain Deuteronomy 14:24-27. | It concerns converting a tithe to money for travel and then rejoicing before the Lord at the chosen place. | He explains that if someone lived too far from the chosen place to transport the tithe, the tithe could be exchanged for money and carried instead. At the destination the person could buy food or drink, rejoice before the Lord with the household, and still remember the Levite who had no inheritance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=7673" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:53</a> | What does Hebrews 7:15-17 mean by a priest in the order of Melchizedek because of an indestructible life? | Hebrews contrasts Christ's eternal priesthood with priesthood based on physical descent. | He says Hebrews is arguing that Christ is greater than the Levitical priesthood. Because Jesus is from Judah, not Levi or Aaron's family, his priesthood is not based on the law's physical descent requirement but on the Melchizedek pattern of a king-priest and on the power of an indestructible life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=8168" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:08</a> | If Palestine lacked a deep sea port, how did Dan have ships in Judges 5:17? | Dan had Joppa, a real port, though not originally a deep sea port. | He distinguishes between a shallow port and a deep sea port. Dan had Joppa, which was suitable for fishing fleets and later became an important port, even though the coast's major engineered deep sea harbor was Caesarea under Herod. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=8384" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:44</a> | Did ancient Egypt have elite warriors called the Medjai, as in The Mummy? | Not as the film presents them. Medjai existed, but by Dynasty 19 they were more like guards, police, or officials. | He says the film uses a real name but not an accurate role. The Medjai existed and earlier served as mercenaries or border patrols, but by the period associated with Seti I they were community leaders, guards, constables, or police officers rather than elite warriors. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=8682" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:42</a> | Why are many scholars convinced the Song of Deborah is the oldest part of the Bible and dates to the 1200s BC? | He disputes the 1200s date and criticizes the assumption that poetic archaism proves earlier composition. | He says the Song of Deborah probably belongs closer to the 1100s than the 1200s and criticizes the reasoning that poetry must predate prose because it uses archaisms. He points to Egyptian documents that combine prose and poetry in the same composition, arguing that poetic archaism does not prove an earlier date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=9009" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:09</a> | What about gradual unveiling of God's character to explain harsh judgments, Jonah, and Jesus? | Yes; theologians call this progressive revelation. | He says the general concept is standard theological language for progressive revelation: God does not reveal everything at once but adds revelation over time. He qualifies that some Old Testament judgments should be called culturally appropriate rather than simply harsh, while Jonah's mercy theme and Jesus as culmination still fit the progressive-revelation framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=9203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:23</a> | Have you read Nigro's recent preliminary report? | He asks which one; he has read multiple 2023 Jericho reports but not every recent one. | He says Nigro has produced several preliminary reports, so the question needs a specific report. He has read two 2023 Jericho reports, one on Early Bronze Age Jericho and one on Late Bronze Age Jericho, but says he had not read the 2024 report being mentioned. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=9267" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:27</a> | Is Tel Heshbon a problem for a 13th-century conquest? | Not necessarily; he treats it as an absence-of-evidence issue that raises questions but is not decisive. | He says the lack of Late Bronze II evidence at Tel Heshbon raises a question but is not decisive by itself. Possible explanations include the site moving, as happens elsewhere, or later leveling removing relevant remains, so he treats it as an absence-of-evidence problem rather than a conclusive disproof. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=9836" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:56</a> | Why would Elizabeth keep herself secluded in Luke 1:24? | He does not treat it as a Levitical requirement and suggests she may have wanted to avoid shame or accusations until the pregnancy was clear. | He notes that Levitical law does not require a pregnant woman to seclude herself, so he looks to Elizabeth's comment about God removing her disgrace. His tentative explanation is that, after barrenness and possible social suspicion, she may have waited until the pregnancy was unmistakable before appearing publicly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=10423" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:43</a> | When educated people disagree, how can a layperson discern truth and speak confidently? | Go back to Scripture, compare the options, and remember experts can be wrong. | He advises returning to Scripture, comparing the interpretations, consulting commentaries and multiple sources, and asking what biases or presuppositions may be involved. He stresses that experts are human and can be wrong, so discernment can be difficult but still belongs to the reader. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=11049" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:09</a> | Any book or video recommendations for learning to think like an Old Testament Israelite or New Testament Jew? | He recommends John Walton's Lost World books. | He recommends John Walton's Lost World series as a primer for entering the ancient Israelite context. He names examples such as the Lost World books on Genesis, the prophets, and the judges, and says Walton does a good job with ancient context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=11124" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:24</a> | How similar is the creator god Ptah to Yahweh in the Bible? | Only in the idea of fiat creation; otherwise he sees little similarity. | He says the only real similarity is fiat creation, meaning creation by thought or speech, as in Ptah creating by mind and voice and Yahweh saying "Let there be light." Beyond that he emphasizes differences: Yahweh is transcendent and bodiless, while Ptah is immanent within creation and depicted bodily as a mummy-like figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=11280" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:00</a> | How would the disciples have understood Mark 8:34 before the crucifixion? | Very poorly; taking up a cross would have sounded shameful and horrifying before the crucifixion. | He says the command would have landed badly before the crucifixion because a cross meant execution. To a first-century Israelite, "take up your cross" would sound like giving up everything and carrying the instrument of your death, so it would be shocking rather than inspirational. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=11907" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:18:27</a> | What would be the point of claiming to be the Antichrist? | In his view, the claim would be a twisted attempt to present oneself as both Christ and Antichrist, fulfilling both roles. | He says people who claim to be the Antichrist often also claim to be Christ or a messiah figure. The twisted logic is to present oneself as the consummation of both sides, combining peaceful Christ imagery with a dark or powerful opposite in a quasi-dualistic way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=12069" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:09</a> | Will the Antichrist be a Jew? | It is possible, but he says the Bible does not tell us and there is no need to insist on it. | He says a Jewish Antichrist is possible but not required by the text. Speculation about Dan is only an inference, and he emphasizes that Scripture does not specify the ethnicity, though the figure will be anti-Christian, anti-Semitic, cunning, and not likely to openly announce himself as Antichrist. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=12324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:24</a> | How similar are the fights and squabbles of Egyptian gods to Greek mythology? | Not very similar. Egyptian divine conflict is comparatively limited and context-specific. | He says Egyptian divine conflict is much more limited than Greek mythological infighting. The main examples he gives are Horus and Seth contesting kingship and the daily battle of the sun god against Apophis, not the constant divine intrigue and battlefield conflict found in Greek stories. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=12429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:27:09</a> | Did Egyptian gods physically get women pregnant like the Olympians? | Generally no, though Egyptian royal birth theology gives gods a role in the conception of kings. | He says ordinary conceptions were not explained this way, unlike some Greek stories used to account for unwed pregnancy. The exception is royal birth theology, where gods such as Khnum, Amun-Re, and Mut are involved in forming or implanting the king, but this applies to kingship ideology rather than ordinary women. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=12744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:24</a> | In 1 Kings 3:2, would one of the high places be Mount Ebal? | No. He says the Mount Ebal site was remote and short-lived. | He says Mount Ebal had potential to become a high place but lasted only about fifty years and had shut down long before Solomon. Since 1 Kings 3 is roughly two centuries after the conquest, he thinks the high places mentioned there must be other sites. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=13110" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:30</a> | Are there ancient Near Eastern documents composed the way source critics propose the Pentateuch was composed? | Not really. The Book of the Dead is the closest loose comparison, but he rejects that as a parallel for documentary-hypothesis redaction. | He says he is unaware of any document composed by repeated source mashing and redaction in the way the documentary hypothesis proposes. The Egyptian Book of the Dead has a long textual history from Pyramid Texts to Coffin Texts to codified spells, but he sees that as copying, selection, and organic modification, not the same kind of redaction model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=13462" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:22</a> | Is the documentary hypothesis still the mainstream view in scholarship? | Not globally. He says it remains the majority view in North America, but not in Europe or Israel, where the supplementary hypothesis has largely supplanted it. | He says the documentary hypothesis remains dominant mainly in North America, while Europe and Israel have largely moved toward the supplementary hypothesis. He criticizes the documentary approach for lacking objective criteria for redaction, noting that proposed markers have shifted from divine names to doublets and other subjective features. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=13892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:32</a> | Are there guides to the Ante-Nicene Fathers' writings that you recommend? | He recommends J. N. D. Kelly's Early Christian Doctrines as a primer. | He says J. N. D. Kelly's Early Christian Doctrines is a good primer on the early church and its doctrinal development. He recommends it because it makes frequent reference to the church fathers and can orient readers before they go deeper into Ante-Nicene writings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=13994" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:53:14</a> | Where can I find more recent translations of lesser-known church fathers? | He mentions newer translation series but cannot recall the specific series name during the stream. | He says there are newer translation series, including a Catholic University Press series, but he cannot recall the exact title during the stream. He searches his electronic library while answering and concludes he would need to follow up later because he cannot safely name the series offhand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=14303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:58:23</a> | Who got Kenneth Kitchen's library? | Tyndale House got Kitchen's library. | He says Tyndale House received Kitchen's library and calls that a major gain for the institution. He adds that his own bigger question is who received Kitchen's photographs and slides, because those contained many Ramesside inscriptions, some of which no longer survive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=14543" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:23</a> | Is there ancient Near Eastern material on poltergeist activity? | Yes. He recommends The First Ghosts by Irving Finkel. | He recommends Irving Finkel's The First Ghosts as the source to consult. He says it discusses Mesopotamian evidence for phenomena like objects being thrown around, which he identifies as the basic definition of poltergeist activity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=14659" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:19</a> | Are lines between specific clans set in stone or subjective? | They can be subjective; clans subdivide and can split into new clans over time. | He explains that clans divide into clades and then families, and that a clade can become a separate clan, especially if a prominent member becomes famous or important. A clan's end is less subjective when the last member dies, but beginnings and divisions can be socially fluid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=14934" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:54</a> | Are hauntings with ghosts of dead people actual people or demons masquerading as people? | He discusses several possibilities, including living-person manifestations, place memories, and masquerading spirits; he doubts ordinary dead-person spirits are the best explanation. | He lays out several categories: hauntings by living people, spirits of the dead, engrammatic or place-memory manifestations, and spirits masquerading as people. He finds ordinary dead-person spirits the least compelling explanation, but says some accounts may be true manifestations and must be evaluated by their circumstances. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=15247" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:07</a> | What even are ghosts? | He treats ghosts as apparitions or perceived nonphysical beings or remnants that may be seen, heard, felt, or otherwise experienced. | He defines a ghost as an apparition: something perceived by a human as a nonphysical being or remnant of a being. It may be seen, heard, felt, or otherwise experienced, and he leaves several possible explanations open while doubting that ghosts are normally returning spirits of the dead. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=15727" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:22:07</a> | Have ghosts, spirits, or demons been recorded on camera? | He says direct recording is doubtful for noncorporeal beings, though physical effects associated with poltergeist claims could be recorded. | He says some activity has allegedly been recorded, but such recordings are contested. Since noncorporeal beings would need the human mind to be visible, he doubts cameras would capture them directly, though physical effects such as objects moving in poltergeist cases could be recorded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=15829" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:23:49</a> | Can cats and bunnies get along? | Sometimes, depending on the individual animals and their relative size; he says to be careful and especially wary of dogs. | He says cats and rabbits sometimes coexist well and sometimes do not, depending on the individual animals and their sizes. He would trust a cat with a rabbit more than a dog, because dogs and rabbits are more directly predator and prey, but still recommends caution. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=15937" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:37</a> | Thoughts on the new pope? | It is too early to tell; he has heard the pope is moderate and hopes he respects church tradition. | He says he does not know enough yet to give a firm view. What he has heard is that the pope is moderate, likely to follow some lines of Pope Francis while trying to respect church tradition, so he hopes for better but says only time will tell. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=16013" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:26:53</a> | In Joshua 10:41 and 11:16, did Goshen move? | No. He explains this as a duplicate toponym: one name can refer to more than one place or region. | He says this is not a case of the Egyptian Goshen moving, but of duplicate place names. One Goshen is in the Nile Delta and another is in the Levant, and he compares this to repeated names such as Kadesh, Antioch, Hermopolis, or modern American Springfields. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=16226" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:30:26</a> | Do the colors in Exodus 26:1 give clues about how the cherubim were woven in an Egyptian context? | Not much. The colors suggest possible pigments or dyes, but not enough to reconstruct the weaving. | He says the colors do not reveal much about the weaving method, though they do raise possibilities such as Egyptian blue, Egyptian purple, scarlet insect dye, or crushed turquoise as pigments or dyes. He explains Egyptian blue as a copper-based artificial pigment but says the color list alone cannot reconstruct the cherubim's manufacture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=16436" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:33:56</a> | Is it UV light that makes the scarlet pigment fade? | No. He says dehydration is the issue. | He says the fading is not caused by ultraviolet light but by dehydration. He compares it to hydrated copper sulfate crystals, where the blue color is tied to water in the compound and the material turns white as it loses water. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/wf4G-NcyCe4?t=16506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:35:06</a> | If Egyptian blue is used in resin, would it maintain its color? | Probably yes, and he discusses possible infrared luminescence behavior. | He thinks embedding Egyptian blue in resin or glass would likely preserve the color and wonders about an experiment using its infrared luminescence. He says shining red light on Egyptian blue produces infrared radiation, so embedding it in a clear medium might have interesting optical applications, though that goes beyond the tabernacle question. |
